title: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.title: Understanding Heart Failure Definitions for Accurate Diagnosis
items.content: Heart failure (HF) is defined by the AHA/ACC/HFSA as a syndrome with symptoms and signs due to ventricular filling or ejection impairment. The ESC emphasizes cardinal symptoms like breathlessness, ankle swelling, and fatigue, along with elevated jugular venous pressure, pulmonary crackles, and peripheral edema. An international consensus offers a universal definition encompassing disease stages, prognostic validity, and sensitivity.
keyPoints: HF is a syndrome from structural or functional ventricular impairment.
ESC highlights breathlessness, ankle swelling, fatigue, elevated jugular venous pressure, pulmonary crackles, peripheral edema.
Universal definition includes disease stages with prognostic and therapeutic validity.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.title: Differentiating Chronic and Acute Heart Failure for Clinical Management
items.content: Chronic heart failure involves longstanding symptoms managed medically or with devices, with remission preferred over stable HF due to ongoing risk. Acute heart failure indicates rapid onset or worsening of symptoms, often from chronic HF exacerbation (~80%) or new-onset HF (~20%), seen in acute coronary syndrome or hypertensive urgency.
keyPoints: Chronic HF involves longstanding symptoms, managed with medical and device therapy.
Remission is preferred over stable HF due to ongoing risk of decompensation.
Acute HF has rapid onset/worsening; ~20% are new-onset cases.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.title: Understanding the Rising Prevalence of Heart Failure (HF)
items.content: Heart failure is a significant global health issue with increasing prevalence, especially among older adults. The lifetime risk of developing HF has risen to 24%, and projections indicate that the number of Americans living with HF will reach 8.5 million by 2030.
keyPoints: 6.7 million American adults are being treated for HF.
>600,000 new cases diagnosed each year in the U.S.
Global prevalence is estimated at 56.2 million people.
Prevalence increases with age: 1–2% (40-49 years) and >10% (>80 years).
Lifetime risk of HF has increased to 24%.
Projected rise in U.S. prevalence to 8.5 million by 2030.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Heart Failure Epidemiology
items.content: There are significant racial and ethnic differences in the epidemiology of heart failure, with black individuals having the highest risk. These disparities are linked to variations in cardiometabolic risk factors and social determinants such as socioeconomic status and healthcare access.
keyPoints: Black individuals have the highest risk of developing HF.
Order of risk: Black > Hispanic > White > Chinese Americans.
Age-adjusted hospitalization rates are highest for black men, followed by black women, white men, and white women.
HF epidemiology in emerging nations is becoming similar to Western Europe and North America.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Heart Failure Phenotypes
items.content: Heart failure can be classified based on ejection fraction into HFrEF, HFmrEF, and HFpEF. Each phenotype has distinct demographic characteristics, comorbidities, and therapeutic responses.
keyPoints: Approximately 50% of heart failure patients have reduced EF (≤40%), while the other half have preserved EF (≥50%) or mildly reduced EF (41–49%).
Classifying HF by EF is crucial due to differences in demographics, comorbidities, and response to therapies.
HFpEF diagnosis can be challenging as it requires ruling out noncardiac causes of symptoms.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.title: Prognosis and Recovery in Heart Failure
items.content: A subset of patients with HFrEF can experience recovery of EF, termed HFrecEF. These patients often have better prognosis but may remain symptomatic due to persistent diastolic dysfunction or other comorbidities.
keyPoints: Predictors of HFrecEF include younger age, shorter duration of HF, nonischemic etiology, smaller ventricular volumes, and absence of myocardial fibrosis.
Despite recovery of EF, patients may remain symptomatic due to persistent abnormalities in diastolic function or related comorbidities.
Prognosis for HFrecEF is superior to HFrEF or HFpEF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.title: Epidemiology and Economic Impact of Heart Failure
items.content: Heart failure imposes a significant economic burden, with costs rising due to hospitalizations and lost productivity. The prevalence and outcomes vary by race, gender, and region.
keyPoints: The overall cost of HF care in the U.S. was estimated at $22.3 billion in 2018, projected to rise to $70 billion by 2030 for hospitalization costs alone.
Black patients have higher case-fatality rates compared to white patients; men have higher age-adjusted mortality rates than women.
Mortality rate from HF varies by region (highest in Midwest) and population density (highest in rural areas).
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Genetic Testing in Familial Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Familial cardiomyopathies, often inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, necessitate a detailed three-generational family history and clinical genetic testing to identify pathogenic variants.
keyPoints: Inherited cardiomyopathies are increasingly recognized in adults with heart failure (HF).
Common disorders include hypertrophic and arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathies.
Society guidelines emphasize the importance of a detailed three-generational family history.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.title: Diverse Etiologies of Heart Failure with Preserved EF
items.content: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) involves multiple pathophysiologic processes beyond diastolic dysfunction, including vascular stiffness and inflammation.
keyPoints: HFpEF is characterized by diastolic dysfunction as a primary mediator.
Pathophysiological processes include vascular stiffness, renal dysfunction, sodium avidity, and inflammation.
Biological alterations such as oxidative stress and impaired nitric oxide signaling are implicated.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.title: Mechanisms of Ventricular Remodeling in HFrEF
items.content: Ventricular remodeling in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) involves structural changes like concentric and eccentric hypertrophy, leading to progressive disease.
keyPoints: HFrEF is a progressive disease involving structural and functional cardiovascular remodeling.
Concentric hypertrophy occurs under pressure overload conditions, reducing wall stress.
Eccentric hypertrophy occurs in volume overload conditions, increasing cavity size.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.title: Role of Neurohormonal Activation in Heart Failure
items.content: Neurohormonal activation, specifically the sympathetic nervous system and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, is crucial in heart failure development. Initially beneficial by increasing cardiac output, chronic activation leads to detrimental effects such as vasoconstriction, volume retention, myocardial toxicity, and arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Neurohormonal activation increases heart rate, blood pressure, and contractility initially.
Long-term effects include excessive vasoconstriction, volume retention, electrolyte abnormalities, baroreceptor dysfunction, direct myocardial toxicity, and cardiac arrhythmias.
Antagonism of RAAS and SNS with specific inhibitors reduces morbidity and mortality.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.title: Beneficial Effects of Vasodilatory Hormones in Heart Failure
items.content: Counterregulatory hormones like natriuretic peptides, prostaglandins, and nitric oxide are upregulated in heart failure. They provide beneficial effects such as vasodilation, increased sodium excretion, and inhibition of renin and aldosterone.
keyPoints: Natriuretic peptides (ANP and BNP) promote systemic and pulmonary vasodilation, increase sodium and water excretion, inhibit renin and aldosterone, and modulate baroreceptors.
Bradykinin and natriuretic peptides are inactivated by neprilysin, explaining the benefit of angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibition.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Endothelin and Oxidative Stress in Heart Failure
items.content: Endothelin, a potent vasoconstrictor, contributes to myocyte hypertrophy and interstitial fibrosis. While endothelin blockade is not beneficial for left ventricular failure, it aids in treating pulmonary arterial hypertension.
keyPoints: Endothelin causes vasoconstriction and growth-promoting effects, leading to myocardial damage.
Blockade of endothelin is effective for pulmonary arterial hypertension but not for left ventricular heart failure progression.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Electrical Dyssynchrony on Heart Failure
items.content: Electrical dyssynchrony, such as left bundle branch block or intraventricular conduction delay, leads to abnormal ventricular contraction and disease progression in heart failure. Correcting this with pacing can improve outcomes.
keyPoints: Up to one-third of HF patients experience QRS interval prolongation due to electrical dyssynchrony.
Left bundle branch block (LBBB) or intraventricular conduction delay results in abnormal ventricular contraction.
Correction via left or biventricular pacing can improve contractile function, decrease mitral regurgitation, and reverse ventricular remodeling.
Cardiac resynchronization therapy is suggested for symptomatic HFrEF patients with LBBB on guideline-directed medical therapy to reduce morbidity and mortality.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 2)

items.title: Role of Secondary Mitral Regurgitation in Heart Failure Progression
items.content: Secondary mitral regurgitation in HFrEF is due to distortion in the mitral valve apparatus, contributing to HF progression. Interventions targeting this can improve prognosis.
keyPoints: Mitral regurgitation occurs from reduced contractile force and structural changes like spherical ventricle shape and annulus dilation.
These changes lead to decreased leaflet coaptation and increased regurgitant volume, worsening HF.
Transcatheter techniques for mitral valve treatment show promise in selected patients after optimal medical therapy.
Progressive tricuspid regurgitation also promotes adverse right ventricular remodeling, with ongoing studies on transcatheter repair or replacement.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Cardiorenal Syndrome: Beyond Arterial Underfilling
items.content: Cardiorenal syndrome involves the interplay between heart and kidney dysfunction, traditionally attributed to decreased cardiac output leading to renal arterial underperfusion. However, systemic venous congestion and increased backward pressure in heart failure (HF) are also significant contributors to renal dysfunction.
keyPoints: Systemic venous congestion plays a role in cardiorenal syndrome development.
Relief of venous congestion improves renal function in HF.
Increased intraabdominal pressure correlates with renal dysfunction in worsening HF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.title: Role of Splanchnic Congestion and Reflexes in Volume Regulation in HF
items.content: In heart failure, the splanchnic veins act as a blood reservoir, influencing cardiac preload through transmural pressure changes or systemic sympathetic activation. Splanchnic congestion leads to portal vein distension, activating hepatorenal and splenorenal reflexes that induce renal vasoconstriction.
keyPoints: Splanchnic veins regulate cardiac preload during volume status changes.
Portal vein distension activates hepatorenal and splenorenal reflexes.
Decongestion via diuretics or ultrafiltration improves renal function by reducing abdominal compartment pressure.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.title: Role of Gut Microbiome in Heart Failure Progression
items.content: Alterations in gut microbial composition and increased intestinal permeability contribute to systemic inflammation in heart failure (HF). Elevated levels of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from gram-negative bacteria stimulate immune responses, leading to macrophage activation and cytokine release. This inflammatory cascade can exacerbate cardiac dysfunction and promote cardiac cachexia.
keyPoints: Proinflammatory cytokines are elevated in HF states.
Gut microbial composition changes may lead to inflammation and immune dysregulation.
LPS levels increase with intestinal permeability during congestion, reduced by diuretics.
Cytokines like TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-6 contribute to cardiac function loss and cachexia.
Trimethylamine N-oxide from dietary nutrients links gut microbes to poor HF outcomes.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.title: High-Output Heart Failure and Its Triggers
items.content: In heart failure (HF), high-output states can precipitate HF when underlying cardiovascular disease is present. Conditions like chronic anemia, pulmonary disease, severe obesity, and others can lead to increased cardiac output with low systemic vascular resistance. Anemia-induced high CO occurs at hemoglobin levels ≤8 g/dL due to vasodilation from decreased oxygen-carrying capacity.
keyPoints: High-output states are rare causes of HF but can precipitate it in underlying disease.
Anemia leads to high CO with low SVR when hemoglobin is ≤8 g/dL.
Common causes of high-output HF include pulmonary disease (19.8%), severe obesity (9.9%), and sepsis (9.6%).
End-stage renal disease patients are at risk due to chronic anemia and arteriovenous fistula.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.title: Understanding Dyspnea in Heart Failure
items.content: Dyspnea is a cardinal manifestation of left heart failure, arising from pulmonary venous congestion and fluid transudation into the interstitium or alveoli. It can manifest as exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND), and dyspnea at rest.
keyPoints: Mechanisms include decreased lung compliance, increased airway resistance, hypoxemia, and ventilation/perfusion mismatch.
Juxtacapillary J receptors stimulation can cause lactic acidosis and a sensation of dyspnea.
NYHA functional classification categorizes breathlessness but does not correlate well with cardiac structure or function.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Orthopnea in Heart Failure
items.content: Orthopnea is dyspnea that occurs when lying down, due to fluid redistribution and increased work of breathing. It typically manifests within 1-2 minutes of recumbency and can be relieved by elevating the head.
keyPoints: Occurs with fluid redistribution from abdomen/lower body into chest.
May be accompanied by nocturnal cough related to pulmonary congestion.
Severe cases may lead patients to sleep in a recliner or sitting up.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea
items.content: PND involves sudden episodes of shortness of breath awakening the patient from sleep, requiring them to sit upright for relief. It is less predictable than orthopnea and may be accompanied by coughing and wheezing.
keyPoints: Occurs after prolonged recumbency, often taking 30 minutes or longer in an upright position for relief.
Associated with increased bronchial arterial pressure leading to airway compression and interstitial pulmonary edema.
Cheyne-Stokes respiration and central sleep apnea can precipitate PND episodes.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Identify Precipitating Factors in Heart Failure
items.content: Recognizing precipitating factors is crucial for managing heart failure, as they can lead to decompensation by increasing cardiac workload. These factors are identifiable in a significant portion of admissions and include patient-related, provider-related, disease-specific, and other causes.
keyPoints: Precipitating factors increase cardiac workload leading to decompensation.
Identifiable in 50–90% of heart failure admissions.
Categories: patient-related, provider-related, HF-related disease states, and other causes.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Assess General Appearance for Severity of Heart Failure
items.content: The general appearance of patients with heart failure can indicate the severity of their condition. Mild to moderate cases may appear well-nourished and comfortable at rest, while severe cases show signs like anxiety, diaphoresis, dyspnea, pallor, cool extremities, peripheral cyanosis, and cardiac cachexia.
keyPoints: Mild-moderate HF patients appear well-nourished and comfortable at rest.
Severe HF may present with anxiety, diaphoresis, dyspnea, pallor, cool extremities, and peripheral cyanosis.
Cardiac cachexia: >5% unintentional edema-free weight loss over 12 months.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia
items.content: Cachexia diagnosis involves identifying an underlying disease with significant body weight loss and additional clinical criteria.
keyPoints: Body weight loss ≥5% in ≤12 months or BMI <20 kg/m²
Plus at least three of five criteria: decrease in muscle strength, fatigue, anorexia, low fat-free mass index, abnormal biochemistry (inflammation, anemia, low serum albumin levels)
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Features of Heart Failure
items.content: Heart failure presents with specific clinical features that aid in diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: Resting heart rate ideally <70–75 beats/min, blood pressure normal to low-normal range
Irregular rhythm may indicate atrial fibrillation or flutter
Severe HF: hypotension, narrow pulse pressure, rapid thready pulse
Pulsus alternans due to reduced left ventricular contraction every other beat
Respiratory rate increases on lying down or minimal exertion
Advanced HF may show periodic breathing or Cheyne-Stokes respirations
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.title: Jugular Venous Pulse Examination in Heart Failure
items.content: Jugular venous pressure assessment provides insights into right atrial pressure and heart failure severity.
keyPoints: Examine at a $45^{	ext{o}}$ angle, quantify JVP by estimating height above sternal angle + 5 cm H₂O
Normal JVP ≤8 cm H₂O; increases with right upper quadrant compression in mild HF
Hepatogujular reflux indicates higher venous pressure levels
Prominent V waves and Y descents suggest significant tricuspid regurgitation
Abdominojugular test suggests elevated left-sided filling pressure
Elevation in JVP during inspiration or Kussmaul's sign indicates severe biventricular HF
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.title: Significance of Hepatomegaly in Right Heart Failure
items.content: Hepatomegaly is an early indicator of systemic venous congestion and can be associated with right heart failure. Initially, the liver edge may be tender due to capsule stretching but tends to lose tenderness as right HF progresses. Additionally, a pulsatile liver edge might indicate tricuspid regurgitation.
keyPoints: Hepatomegaly is an early sign of systemic venous congestion.
Tenderness of the liver edge may disappear with progression of right heart failure (HF).
A pulsatile liver edge can be a sign of tricuspid regurgitation.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Management of Lower Extremity Edema
items.content: Lower extremity edema is common in chronic heart failure, typically presenting as symmetric and pitting. However, unilateral edema may suggest deep venous thrombosis or other causes like prior trauma or vein harvest for bypass surgery. Nonpitting edema unresponsive to diuretics suggests lymphedema, necessitating a different diagnostic approach.
keyPoints: Dependent lower extremity edema is common in chronic HF and usually symmetric and pitting.
Unilateral lower extremity edema may indicate deep venous thrombosis or other causes such as prior trauma or vein harvest for bypass surgery.
Nonpitting edema unresponsive to diuretics suggests lymphedema, requiring alternative diagnostic workup.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Laboratory Testing in HF Diagnosis
items.content: Laboratory testing is crucial for diagnosing heart failure (HF), especially when patients present with non-specific symptoms. Key tests include a comprehensive metabolic panel, complete blood count, and urinalysis. Specific conditions like worsening renal function or proteinuria may indicate underlying systemic diseases.
keyPoints: Routine labs: comprehensive metabolic panel, complete blood count, coagulation studies, urinalysis.
Elevated BUN/creatinine due to reduced renal blood flow/increased venous pressure.
Proteinuria suggests longstanding hypertension/diabetes or systemic disease.
Hyponatremia is a negative prognostic indicator and predicts decreased survival.
Hypokalemia often from diuretics, hyperkalemia from RAAS inhibitors.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Electrolyte Monitoring in HF
items.content: Electrolyte imbalances are common in HF and have significant clinical implications. Hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and hyperkalemia can all occur due to various factors such as diuretic use or RAAS inhibitors, potentially leading to arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Hyponatremia indicates poor prognosis during HF hospitalization.
Hypokalemia often results from thiazide or loop diuretics without potassium supplementation.
Hyperkalemia can be exacerbated by RAAS inhibitors and reduced glomerular filtration rate.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Significance of Chest X-Ray in HF Diagnosis
items.content: Chest x-ray is a valuable tool for diagnosing left heart failure by identifying enlarged cardiac silhouette and pulmonary venous congestion. It can also help differentiate between cardiac and noncardiac causes of dyspnea.
keyPoints: Enlarged cardiac silhouette (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5) indicates left HF.
Pulmonary venous congestion is a major abnormality in left HF.
Chest x-ray can identify noncardiac causes like pneumonia or COPD.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Noninvasive Imaging in HF Diagnosis
items.content: Noninvasive imaging techniques are crucial for diagnosing and managing heart failure (HF). Echocardiography is pivotal for assessing ventricular size, function, and valvular morphology. It helps determine left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), with a normal systolic function defined as LVEF ≥ 50%. Doppler techniques estimate cardiac output and pulmonary pressures, while CMR provides detailed evaluation of ventricular mass and specific HF causes.
keyPoints: Echocardiography assesses ventricular size/function; normal LVEF is ≥ 50%.
Doppler estimates CO, pulmonary artery pressures, valve areas.
CMR evaluates left ventricular mass/volumes/functions; useful for ischemic cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, amyloidosis, hemochromatosis.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Cardiopulmonary Exercise Testing in HF
items.content: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing is not routine but provides objective assessment of peak functional capacity, particularly for patients evaluated for mechanical circulatory support or heart transplant. It measures parameters like peak oxygen consumption (VO2) and ventilatory efficiency (VE/VCO2 slope), which are independent predictors of survival.
keyPoints: Peak VO2 and VE/VCO2 slope predict survival in HF.
Assesses heart rate, blood pressure response to exercise; detects exercise-induced arrhythmias.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Use of Biomarkers in HF Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Biomarkers such as BNP and NT-proBNP are adjunctive tools for diagnosing heart failure. They reflect increased wall stress from the atria and ventricles, with higher levels typically seen in HFrEF compared to HFpEF. These biomarkers support clinical decision-making in ambiguous cases or when lung disease is present.
keyPoints: BNP/NT-proBNP levels correlate with heart failure severity; normal LVEF can still have elevated levels.
Noncardiac factors like age, sex, and kidney disease affect natriuretic peptide levels.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Role of SGLT-2 Inhibitors in Heart Failure and Diabetes Coexistence
items.content: SGLT-2 inhibitors are beneficial for patients with both heart failure (HF) and diabetes, improving outcomes beyond glucose control.
keyPoints: Prevalence of diabetes in ambulatory HF cohorts ranges from 10 to 40%.
Coexistence increases risk for adverse outcomes, worse quality of life, and higher care costs.
SGLT-2 inhibitors improve renal function, enhance quality of life, increase LVEF, and decrease hospitalization and death risk.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | DIABETES

items.title: Importance of Differentiating Cardiogenic from Noncardiogenic Pulmonary Edema in HF
items.content: Invasive studies like pulmonary artery catheterization are crucial for differentiating cardiogenic from noncardiogenic pulmonary edema and managing hemodynamic instability.
keyPoints: Pulmonary artery catheter placement is safe at the bedside.
Used to assess response to intravenous vasoactive and diuretic therapy in severe HF.
Simultaneous measurement of right and left heart filling pressures helps distinguish restrictive cardiomyopathy from constrictive pericarditis.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | DIABETES

items.title: Sleep Apnea in Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Management
items.content: Sleep-disordered breathing, including obstructive and central sleep apnea, is prevalent in heart failure (HF) patients. The pathophysiological mechanisms involve increased afterload, decreased preload, intermittent hypoxia, and sympathetic activation, which can lead to ischemia, arrhythmias, and blood pressure management challenges. Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) improves outcomes in HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and obstructive sleep apnea but no proven therapy exists for central sleep apnea.
keyPoints: Increased incidence of both obstructive and central sleep apnea in HF patients
Pathophysiological mechanisms: increased afterload, decreased preload, intermittent hypoxia, sympathetic activation
Approximately one-third of HF patients with sleep-disordered breathing have central sleep apnea
Central sleep apnea is associated with increased mortality independent of other risk factors
CPAP improves quality of life, decreases blood pressure and arrhythmias, increases left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) in HFrEF with obstructive sleep apnea
No proven therapy for central sleep apnea; phrenic or hypoglossal nerve pacing may benefit some cases
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · SLEEP APNEA | OBESITY | ☐ DEPRESSION

items.title: Obesity and Heart Failure: Risk, Prognosis, and Management
items.content: Obesity is a risk factor for heart failure (HF) development and prevalent in HF patients, particularly those with HFpEF. It complicates volume status assessment and presents the obesity paradox where obese HF patients have better prognosis than those with lower BMI. Weight loss improves quality of life and exercise capacity but its impact on survival is unknown. GLP-1 agonists like semaglutide are being studied for safety and efficacy in HF with obesity.
keyPoints: Obesity is a risk factor for HF development and prevalent in HF patients, especially HFpEF
Complicates volume status assessment in both ambulatory and inpatient settings
Obesity paradox: obese HF patients have more favorable prognosis than those with low or normal BMI
Weight loss improves quality of life and exercise capacity; may contribute to reverse ventricular remodeling
Impact of weight loss on survival is unknown
GLP-1 agonists (e.g., semaglutide) being assessed in clinical trials for safety and efficacy in HF with obesity
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · SLEEP APNEA | OBESITY | ☐ DEPRESSION

items.title: Distinguishing Dyspnea in HF from Pulmonary Causes
items.content: In patients presenting with dyspnea, it is crucial to differentiate between cardiac and pulmonary causes. This differentiation can be challenging as symptoms like orthopnea may overlap between severe chronic lung disease and heart failure (HF). In pulmonary diseases, episodic shortness of breath during sleep typically results from tracheobronchial secretions and improves with coughing, whereas in HF, sitting upright is necessary for relief.
keyPoints: Orthopnea can occur in both severe chronic lung disease and HF.
Episodic nocturnal dyspnea due to secretion accumulation differs from HF-related symptoms which improve by sitting upright.
Wheezing in reactive airways disease may worsen with left ventricular failure, presenting as cardiac asthma.
Cardiac asthma often shows diaphoresis and cyanosis more than bronchial asthma.
Pulmonary function tests post-diuresis can help identify the primary cause of dyspnea.
Cardiopulmonary exercise testing is useful in ambulatory patients with advanced HF to distinguish causes of dyspnea.
A very low BNP or NT-proBNP level may exclude HF as a cause of dyspnea in nonobese patients.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Identifying Non-HF Causes of Congestion and Edema
items.content: When diagnosing heart failure (HF), it is important to consider other conditions that may cause congestion or edema due to abnormal salt and water retention, even when cardiac structure and function are normal. These include acute or chronic kidney disease. Additionally, noncardiac causes of pulmonary edema such as acute respiratory distress syndrome should be considered. Non-HF causes of lower extremity edema like venous insufficiency, lymphedema, and obesity must also be ruled out.
keyPoints: HF must be differentiated from conditions with congestion due to salt and water retention but normal cardiac function (e.g., kidney disease).
Noncardiac causes of pulmonary edema include acute respiratory distress syndrome.
Consider non-HF causes of lower extremity edema such as venous insufficiency, lymphedema, and obesity.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.question: According to the AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline, how is heart failure (HF) defined?
options: A syndrome with symptoms and signs resulting from any structural or functional impairment of ventricular filling or ejection of blood.
A condition characterized solely by elevated jugular venous pressure and peripheral edema.
A disorder primarily caused by myocardial infarction without other contributing factors.
An acute onset of breathlessness and fatigue due to high cardiac output.
items.explanation: The AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline defines HF as a syndrome with symptoms and signs from any structural or functional impairment of ventricular filling or ejection of blood.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.question: What does the ESC definition emphasize in heart failure?
options: The presence of elevated jugular venous pressure alone.
Cardinal symptoms like breathlessness, ankle swelling, and fatigue along with signs due to structural/functional abnormalities.
Only the inadequate cardiac output during exercise without considering rest conditions.
Structural abnormalities without any functional implications.
items.explanation: The ESC definition emphasizes cardinal symptoms (e.g., breathlessness, ankle swelling, and fatigue) with signs due to structural/functional heart abnormalities.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.question: What is the preferred term over 'congestive heart failure' for patients without volume overload signs or symptoms?
items.explanation: 'Heart failure' is preferred over 'congestive heart failure' for patients without signs or symptoms of volume overload.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.question: What is the estimated number of American adults currently being treated for heart failure (HF)?
items.explanation: An estimated 6.7 million American adults are being treated for HF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: By what year is the prevalence of heart failure expected to rise to approximately 8.5 million Americans?
items.explanation: Projections show that the prevalence of HF is expected to rise to 8.5 million Americans by 2030.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which racial group has the highest risk of developing heart failure according to community-based studies?
items.explanation: In community-based studies, black individuals have the highest risk of developing HF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which demographic group has the highest age-adjusted mortality rate for heart failure-related deaths?
items.explanation: Men have higher age-adjusted mortality rates for death related to HF than women.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: What is the estimated global economic burden of heart failure in 2012?
items.explanation: The global economic burden of HF in 2012 was estimated at $108 billion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: Which classification requires evidence of increased left ventricular filling pressures?
items.explanation: The AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline requires evidence of increased left ventricular filling pressures for HFmrEF classification.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: What is a predictor of heart failure with recovered ejection fraction?
items.explanation: Predictors of HFrecEF include younger age, among other factors.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with high-output heart failure in the absence of underlying structural heart disease?
items.explanation: High-output HF is seldom associated with underlying structural heart disease but can occur in conditions like anemia.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.question: What type of hypertrophy results from pressure overload and effectively reduces wall stress?
items.explanation: Concentric hypertrophy occurs under conditions of pressure overload and reduces wall stress.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF)?
items.explanation: Acute myocardial infarction is more commonly associated with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF).
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms is involved in the progression of HFrEF?
items.explanation: Compensatory mechanisms initially help maintain cardiac performance but ultimately contribute to disease progression.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following is a primary effect of natriuretic peptides (ANP and BNP) in heart failure?
items.explanation: Natriuretic peptides mediate systemic and pulmonary vasodilation, among other beneficial effects.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of endothelin in heart failure?
options: It acts as a vasodilator and reduces myocardial hypertrophy.
It promotes vasoconstriction and myocyte hypertrophy.
It decreases sodium and water retention.
items.explanation: Endothelin is a potent vasoconstrictor that contributes to myocyte hypertrophy and interstitial fibrosis.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following therapies has not been shown to slow the progression of clinical heart failure due to left ventricular failure?
items.explanation: Endothelin blockade has not been shown to slow the progression of clinical HF due to left ventricular failure.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.question: What is a beneficial effect of SGLT-2 inhibitors in heart failure?
items.explanation: SGLT-2 inhibitors have been shown to reduce morbidity and mortality in heart failure patients.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.question: Which intervention is suggested for patients with symptomatic HFrEF and LBBB to reduce morbidity and mortality?
options: Cardiac resynchronization therapy or LBBB pacing
items.explanation: Cardiac resynchronization therapy or LBBB (or His bundle) pacing is suggested for these patients.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 2)

items.question: What contributes to worsening heart failure due to secondary mitral regurgitation?
options: Contraction of the mitral annulus during systole
items.explanation: Reduced contractile force leads to decreased coaptation of the leaflets, contributing to secondary mitral regurgitation.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 2)

items.question: What is a therapeutic target in heart failure related to secondary mitral regurgitation?
options: Interrupting the vicious cycle of worsening regurgitant volume
items.explanation: Interruption of the vicious cycle involving worsening regurgitant volume is a therapeutic target.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 2)

items.question: Which factor is NOT traditionally considered a cause of the cardiorenal syndrome?
options: Impairment in forward flow leading to decreased renal arterial perfusion
Neurohormonal activation with release of arginine vasopressin
Increased systemic venous congestion leading to backward pressure
Direct myocardial damage causing kidney failure
items.explanation: The passage describes impairment in forward flow, neurohormonal activation, and increased systemic venous congestion as contributors to the cardiorenal syndrome, but not direct myocardial damage.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.question: What is a significant factor in determining the development of the cardiorenal syndrome according to recent evidence?
options: Arterial underfilling and decline in cardiac output
Systemic venous congestion with increased backward pressure
items.explanation: Recent evidence suggests that systemic venous congestion with increased backward pressure plays a role in the development of cardiorenal syndrome.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.question: How does decongestion therapy affect renal function in heart failure?
options: It increases intraabdominal pressure, worsening renal dysfunction.
It reduces volume and decreases pressure within the abdominal compartment.
items.explanation: Decongestion therapy reduces volume and decreases pressure within the abdominal compartment, which can improve or stabilize renal function in heart failure.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.question: Which bacterial product is associated with increased levels in patients with heart failure due to increased intestinal permeability during periods of congestion?
items.explanation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) levels are increased in patients with heart failure due to increased intestinal permeability during periods of congestion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.question: Which cytokines are mentioned as being elevated due to pathways involving gut microbe-dependent generation and contributing to cardiac cachexia?
items.explanation: The cytokines TNF-$\alpha$, IL-1, and IL-6 are elevated due to pathways involving gut microbe-dependent generation.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.question: What is a potential consequence of bowel ischemia or congestion in heart failure patients?
items.explanation: Bowel ischemia or congestion in heart failure patients can lead to bacterial endotoxemia and a proinflammatory state.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.question: Which symptom is characterized by shortness of breath that occurs when bending forward, particularly associated with an increase in cardiac filling pressure?
items.explanation: Bendopnea is the symptom characterized by shortness of breath when bending forward, associated with increased cardiac filling pressure.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What is a common manifestation of right heart failure related to systemic venous congestion?
items.explanation: Right heart failure often manifests as lower extremity edema due to systemic venous congestion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: Which symptom is described by episodes of shortness of breath that awaken a patient suddenly from sleep, requiring sitting upright for relief?
items.explanation: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea involves sudden awakenings from sleep due to shortness of breath, relieved by sitting upright.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical time frame for orthopnea to occur after lying down in patients with heart failure?
items.explanation: Orthopnea typically occurs within 1-2 minutes of lying down in patients with heart failure.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a precipitating factor for heart failure decompensation?
items.explanation: Adequate treatment is not a precipitating factor; rather, failure to recognize and correct precipitating factors can lead to persistent HF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.question: In patients with severe heart failure, which of the following signs might be observed at rest?
options: Pallor due to anemia or duskiness due to low output
items.explanation: Patients with severe HF may appear anxious, diaphoretic, and dyspneic at rest with pallor due to anemia or duskiness from low output.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.question: What is a contributing factor to cardiac cachexia in patients with longstanding, severe heart failure?
options: Increased fat absorption due to bowel wall edema
Enhanced growth hormone–insulin-like growth factor 1 pathway regulation
Improved oral intake due to increased appetite
items.explanation: Cardiac cachexia is partially caused by activation of inflammatory cytokines and dysregulation of the growth hormone–insulin-like growth factor 1 pathway.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT part of the diagnostic criteria for cachexia in adults according to TABLE 264-6?
options: Underlying disease and body weight loss ≥5% in ≤12 months
Abnormal biochemistry: inflammation, anemia, low serum albumin levels
Resting heart rate ideally should be <70–75 beats/min
items.explanation: The resting heart rate criterion is related to chronic heart failure management, not cachexia diagnosis.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following criteria must be present along with an underlying disease and body weight loss ≥5% in ≤12 months to diagnose cachexia?
options: At least two of five specific criteria
At least three of five specific criteria
items.explanation: Cachexia diagnosis requires at least three out of five additional criteria.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.question: Which symptom is included as one of the diagnostic criteria for cachexia?
items.explanation: A decrease in muscle strength is one of the diagnostic criteria for cachexia.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.question: What is a biochemical abnormality associated with cachexia?
items.explanation: Cachexia is associated with low serum albumin levels as part of abnormal biochemistry.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.question: Which condition is indicated by the presence of a pulsatile liver edge?
items.explanation: A pulsatile liver edge is associated with tricuspid regurgitation.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.question: What should be considered if massive ascites are present?
options: Primary liver failure or constrictive pericarditis
items.explanation: Massive ascites should prompt investigation for other causes such as primary liver failure or constrictive pericarditis.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.question: What type of edema is characterized by being massive and generalized, involving the legs, sacrum, and abdominal wall?
items.explanation: Anasarca describes massive, generalized edema involving the legs, sacrum, and abdominal wall.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.question: Which laboratory finding in patients with heart failure is a negative prognostic indicator at the time of hospitalization and predicts decreased long-term survival?
items.explanation: Hyponatremia is a negative prognostic indicator at the time of HF hospitalization and predicts decreased long-term survival.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: In patients with heart failure, what condition can lead to modest elevations in transaminases, alkaline phosphatase, and bilirubin?
options: Chronic right HF with congestive hepatomegaly
Cardiogenic shock with severe low output
items.explanation: Chronic right HF with congestive hepatomegaly can lead to modest elevations in transaminases, alkaline phosphatase, and bilirubin.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: What is a common cause of hyperkalemia in patients with heart failure?
items.explanation: Hyperkalemia may result from marked reductions in glomerular filtration rate and is exacerbated by the use of RAAS inhibitors.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which ECG finding suggests heart failure with preserved ejection fraction due to conditions like hypertension or aortic stenosis?
options: Left ventricular hypertrophy and left atrial enlargement
items.explanation: Left ventricular hypertrophy and left atrial enlargement suggest HFpEF due to hypertension, aortic stenosis, or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality is particularly useful for evaluating specific causes of heart failure such as ischemic cardiomyopathy and amyloidosis?
items.explanation: CMR is highly accurate for evaluating specific causes of HF like ischemic cardiomyopathy and amyloidosis.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: In patients with heart failure, which biomarker levels are typically higher in HFrEF compared to HFpEF?
items.explanation: Patients with HFrEF tend to have higher levels of natriuretic peptides than those with HFpEF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary use of cardiopulmonary exercise testing in heart failure patients?
options: To assess left ventricular ejection fraction
To evaluate peak functional capacity and survival predictors
items.explanation: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing provides an objective assessment of peak functional capacity and is a predictor of survival.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging modality should be used to rule out left atrial appendage thrombus prior to cardioversion?
items.explanation: TEE or cardiac CT with contrast is indicated to rule out left atrial appendage thrombus prior to cardioversion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.question: Which invasive study can be used to differentiate cardiogenic from noncardiogenic pulmonary edema?
options: Placement of a pulmonary artery catheter
items.explanation: A pulmonary artery catheter can assess cardiac filling pressures and CO to differentiate between cardiogenic and noncardiogenic pulmonary edema.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | DIABETES

items.question: What is the role of coronary angiography in patients with left ventricular dysfunction?
options: To exclude ischemic heart disease as a reversible cause
To measure right and left heart filling pressures
items.explanation: Coronary angiography is used to exclude ischemic heart disease as an underlying, potentially reversible cause of left ventricular dysfunction.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | DIABETES

items.question: Which therapy has been shown to improve renal function and decrease the risk of hospitalization in patients with HF?
items.explanation: SGLT-2 inhibitors improve renal function, enhance quality of life, increase LVEF, and decrease the risk of hospitalization and death in patients with HF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | DIABETES

items.question: Which of the following is a proven therapy for obstructive sleep apnea in patients with heart failure?
items.explanation: Continuous positive airway pressure has been shown to improve quality of life, decrease blood pressure and arrhythmias, and increase LVEF in patients with HFrEF and obstructive sleep apnea.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · SLEEP APNEA | OBESITY | ☐ DEPRESSION

items.question: What is the association between central sleep apnea and mortality in heart failure patients?
options: Decreased mortality independent of other risk factors
Increased mortality independent of other known risk factors
Mortality only increased when combined with obstructive sleep apnea
items.explanation: Central sleep apnea is associated with increased mortality independent of other known risk factors.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · SLEEP APNEA | OBESITY | ☐ DEPRESSION

items.question: Which statement best describes the 'obesity paradox' in heart failure patients?
options: Obese patients with HF have a worse prognosis than those with low or normal BMI.
Obese patients with HF have a more favorable prognosis than those with low or normal BMI.
Obesity has no impact on the prognosis of HF patients.
Only underweight patients with HF show improved survival rates.
items.explanation: The obesity paradox refers to the observation that obese patients diagnosed with HF have a more favorable prognosis than patients with low or even normal body mass index.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · SLEEP APNEA | OBESITY | ☐ DEPRESSION

items.question: Which symptom is more likely to be exhibited by patients with cardiac asthma compared to those with bronchial asthma?
options: Episodic shortness of breath during sleep
Diaphoresis and varying degrees of cyanosis
Relief of symptoms by coughing and expectoration
items.explanation: Patients with cardiac asthma are more likely to exhibit diaphoresis and varying degrees of cyanosis compared to those with bronchial asthma.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In patients with dyspnea, which test may help determine the predominant cause after effective diuresis?
items.explanation: Following effective diuresis, pulmonary function tests may help to determine the predominant cause of dyspnea.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: In which condition might a very low BNP or NT-proBNP level be helpful in excluding HF as the cause of dyspnea?
options: Patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome
items.explanation: A very low BNP or NT-proBNP level may be helpful in excluding HF as the cause of dyspnea in nonobese patients.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.statement: Acute heart failure refers to the rapid onset or worsening of symptoms of HF.
items.explanation: Acute heart failure is defined as the rapid onset or worsening of HF symptoms.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.statement: Chronic heart failure patients are considered to be in remission when their episode resolves.
items.explanation: When an episode of chronic HF resolves, the term 'remission' is preferred over 'stable HF'.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.statement: The prevalence of heart failure is expected to decrease by 2030.
items.explanation: The prevalence of HF is expected to rise to 8.5 million Americans by 2030.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Rates of heart failure hospitalizations in the United States increased from 2014 to 2017.
items.explanation: HF hospitalizations rates increased from 2014 to 2017 after declining from 2010 to 2014.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: The overall prognosis for patients with heart failure is worsening due to treatment challenges.
items.explanation: Despite statistics, the overall prognosis for HF patients is improving due to treatment of risk factors and increased use of guideline-directed therapies.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.statement: HF with preserved EF often presents diagnostic challenges.
items.explanation: Diagnosis of HFpEF is challenging due to the need to rule out noncardiac causes of symptoms.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.statement: Rheumatic heart disease is a major cause of HF in young individuals, especially in Africa and Asia.
items.explanation: Rheumatic heart disease remains a significant cause of HF among the young in these regions.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.statement: Inherited cardiomyopathies are typically inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion.
items.explanation: Most forms of familial cardiomyopathy are inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.statement: Inhibition of the renin-angiotensin system does not affect ventricular remodeling.
items.explanation: RAAS inhibition attenuates or reverses ventricular and vascular remodeling.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.statement: Neprilysin inactivation of bradykinin contributes to the clinical benefit of angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibition.
items.explanation: Bradykinin and natriuretic peptides are inactivated by neprilysin, explaining part of the beneficial impact.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.statement: Atrial fibrillation with inadequate rate control can contribute to worsening heart failure.
items.explanation: Atrial fibrillation with inadequate rate control contributes to worsening HF through increased wall stress, neurohormonal activation, and inflammation.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 2)

items.statement: Tricuspid regurgitation does not influence right ventricular remodeling.
items.explanation: Progressive tricuspid regurgitation can result from and promote adverse right ventricular remodeling.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 2)

items.statement: Increased intraabdominal pressure is correlated with improved renal function in worsening HF.
items.explanation: The passage states that increased intraabdominal pressure correlates with renal dysfunction, not improvement.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.statement: Splanchnic congestion can lead to activation of the hepatorenal and splenorenal reflexes.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that splanchnic congestion results in portal vein distension, activating these reflexes.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.statement: High-output states are commonly responsible for heart failure.
items.explanation: High-output states by themselves seldom cause heart failure; they can precipitate it in the presence of underlying cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.statement: Chronic anemia is associated with high cardiac output when hemoglobin levels are significantly reduced.
items.explanation: Chronic anemia can lead to high cardiac output due to decreased oxygen-carrying capacity and compensatory mechanisms.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.statement: The New York Heart Association functional classification correlates well with objective measures of cardiac structure and function.
items.explanation: NYHA class does not correlate well with other objective measures such as left ventricular size or peak oxygen consumption.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Orthopnea is relieved by lying flat without any adjustments.
items.explanation: Orthopnea is typically relieved by raising the head and chest with pillows or an adjustable bed.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with mild-moderate heart failure often appear well nourished and comfortable at rest.
items.explanation: Most patients with mild-moderate HF will appear well nourished and comfortable when at rest.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: In new-onset heart failure, blood pressure typically decreases due to sympathetic activation.
items.explanation: With new-onset HF, blood pressure may initially be increased due to sympathetic activation.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.statement: A BMI <20 kg/m² can be a criterion for diagnosing cachexia.
items.explanation: BMI <20 kg/m² is part of the diagnostic criteria for cachexia.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.statement: Anorexia is not considered a criterion for diagnosing cachexia.
items.explanation: Anorexia is one of the criteria used to diagnose cachexia.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.statement: Dependent lower extremity edema is typically asymmetric in chronic heart failure.
items.explanation: Dependent lower extremity edema in chronic heart failure is typically symmetric and pitting.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.statement: Nonpitting edema that does not respond to diuretics may indicate lymphedema.
items.explanation: Nonpitting edema unresponsive to diuretics suggests lymphedema, requiring alternative diagnostic workup and treatment.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.statement: Inflammatory markers such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein are specific for diagnosing heart failure.
items.explanation: Inflammatory markers like ESR, CRP, and uric acid are nonspecific and do not aid in the diagnosis of HF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Anemia is diagnostic of heart failure.
items.explanation: Anemia is not diagnostic of HF, but it may exacerbate underlying ischemic heart disease or decrease quality of life in patients with HF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.statement: Myocardial strain rate imaging using speckle tracking has no prognostic value.
items.explanation: Speckle tracking carries incremental and prognostic value beyond LVEF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: Cardiac CT is particularly helpful in patients who cannot undergo CMR due to implantable devices.
items.explanation: Cardiac CT is used when CMR is contraindicated, such as with implantable devices.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.statement: The prevalence of diabetes in ambulatory HF cohorts ranges from 10 to 40%.
items.explanation: The passage states that the prevalence of diabetes in ambulatory HF cohorts ranges from 10 to 40%.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | DIABETES

items.statement: Right ventricular endomyocardial biopsy is universally recommended for all patients with HF.
items.explanation: The role of right ventricular endomyocardial biopsy in the management of HF remains controversial and is not universally recommended.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 3) | DIABETES

items.statement: Depression is an independent risk factor for adverse outcomes in heart failure, particularly in older women.
items.explanation: Depression is indeed an independent risk factor for adverse outcomes in HF, especially in older women.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · SLEEP APNEA | OBESITY | ☐ DEPRESSION

items.statement: Weight loss has been proven to improve survival rates in patients with heart failure and obesity.
items.explanation: While weight loss improves quality of life and exercise capacity, its impact on survival is unknown.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · SLEEP APNEA | OBESITY | ☐ DEPRESSION

items.statement: Wheezing due to bronchoconstriction is a prominent symptom when left ventricular failure supervenes in individuals with reactive airways disease.
items.explanation: Wheezing caused by bronchoconstriction may be prominent when left ventricular failure occurs in those with reactive airways disease.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: In patients with chronic lung disease, dyspnea during sleep is usually relieved by sitting upright.
items.explanation: In chronic lung disease, episodic shortness of breath during sleep is usually due to accumulation of tracheobronchial secretions and is relieved by coughing and expectoration.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS
items.assertion: Chronic heart failure describes patients with longstanding symptoms of HF.
items.reason: Such patients are typically treated with medical and device therapy as described in Chap. 265.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why chronic HF involves longstanding symptoms.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · Heart Failure: Pathophysiology and Diagnosis | DEFINITIONS

items.assertion: Patients with heart failure and an ejection fraction between 40-50% are classified as having HFmrEF.
items.reason: These patients often have mild systolic dysfunction but also features of diastolic dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these patients are classified as HFmrEF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.assertion: Coronary artery disease is responsible for approximately one-third of heart failure cases.
items.reason: In developed countries, coronary artery disease contributes significantly to heart failure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; coronary artery disease is responsible for approximately two-thirds of HF cases.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 1)

items.assertion: Ventricular remodeling is a key process in the progression of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
items.reason: Remodeling involves myocyte hypertrophy, interstitial fibrosis, and changes at cellular and molecular levels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.assertion: Diastolic dysfunction is the sole mediator of HFpEF.
items.reason: HFpEF involves multiple pathophysiologic processes beyond diastolic dysfunction, such as vascular stiffness and inflammation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because HFpEF involves more than just diastolic dysfunction.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · PHENOTYPES AND CAUSES (part 2) | PROGRESSIVE DISEASE

items.assertion: SGLT-2 inhibitors have demonstrated safety and efficacy in heart failure.
items.reason: They were initially developed for type 2 diabetes mellitus due to their glycosuric effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.assertion: Endothelin blockade is beneficial for left ventricular heart failure.
items.reason: It has shown to slow progression in clinical trials involving left ventricular failure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; endothelin blockade is not beneficial for left ventricular heart failure.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · MECHANISMS OF DISEASE PROGRESSION (part 1)

items.assertion: Relief of venous congestion is associated with significant improvement in renal function in HF.
items.reason: This occurs because it reduces volume and decreases pressure within the abdominal compartment, improving or stabilizing renal function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · CARDIORENAL AND ABDOMINAL INTERACTIONS

items.subtopic: GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES
items.assertion: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) levels are reduced with diuretic treatment in heart failure patients.
items.reason: Diuretics alleviate congestion, which reduces intestinal permeability and subsequently LPS levels.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · GUT CONGESTION, THE MICROBIOME, AND INFLAMMATION | HIGH-OUTPUT STATES

items.assertion: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea usually occurs after prolonged recumbency.
items.reason: PND is less predictable and may require 30 minutes or longer in the upright position for relief.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why PND occurs after prolonged recumbency.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Central sleep apnea can be treated with positive airway pressure.
items.reason: Positive airway pressure is a treatment for obstructive sleep apnea, not central sleep apnea.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; central sleep apnea has no proven therapy beyond treating heart failure.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Patients with severe heart failure often exhibit cool extremities and peripheral cyanosis.
items.reason: These symptoms are due to poor perfusion associated with low cardiac output in severe HF.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.assertion: Cachexia diagnosis requires a combination of weight loss and additional symptoms.
items.reason: Weight loss alone is insufficient; at least three out of five specific criteria must also be met.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)
items.assertion: Fatigue is a criterion for diagnosing cachexia.
items.reason: Fatigue indicates abnormal biochemistry, which is part of the diagnostic criteria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but fatigue being a criterion is not due to it indicating abnormal biochemistry.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)
items.assertion: In patients with acute heart failure or younger adults with chronic heart failure, lower extremity edema may be absent.
items.reason: This is due to marked systemic venous hypertension.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the absence of lower extremity edema despite systemic venous hypertension does not explain why it occurs.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · TABLE 264-6 Diagnostic Criteria for Cachexia in Adults (part 2)

items.assertion: Hyponatremia is a negative prognostic indicator at the time of heart failure hospitalization.
items.reason: It predicts decreased long-term survival in patients with heart failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Left ventricular hypertrophy on ECG is indicative of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
items.reason: It suggests HFpEF due to conditions like hypertension or aortic stenosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason incorrectly associates left ventricular hypertrophy with HFpEF instead of HFrEF.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Biomarkers like BNP and NT-proBNP are useful in diagnosing heart failure.
items.reason: They are released from the atria and ventricles in response to increased wall stress.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why these biomarkers are useful for HF diagnosis.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Coronary CT angiography is not useful in evaluating heart failure.
items.reason: It can rule out obstructive coronary artery disease as a cause of HF.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Coronary CT angiography is indeed useful for ruling out coronary artery disease.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.assertion: Differentiating dyspnea related to HF versus pulmonary disease may be impossible when the diseases coexist.
items.reason: This situation is common in chronically ill older patients with active or prior smoking.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why differentiating dyspnea related to HF versus pulmonary disease may be impossible.
items.reference: Heart Failure Pathophysiology and Diagnosis · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
